one_line_takeaway: Safety-aligned LLMs (including GPT-5.2 and Gemini-3-Pro) routinely reproduce or elaborate on harmful content embedded as 'user-supplied material' inside otherwise benign tasks like translation (up to 79.6% harmful-response rate), because task-level refusal training does not transfer to content-level discernment, and this gap can be exploited to bypass external moderation safeguards by wrapping harmful text inside benign text, degrading safeguard effectiveness by at least 0.25.
key_finding: Nearly all evaluated LLMs are substantially vulnerable to in-content harm risk. Weighted-average K-HRN/T-HRR (Table 2): GPT-3.5 Turbo 4.035/0.448 (highest closed-model risk), Qwen3 3.942/0.438, Vicuna 3.868/0.432, Gemini-3-Pro 3.498/0.389, GPT-5.2 3.195/0.355, GPT-4 Turbo 1.905/0.217, Gemma 1.061/0.118, Llama2 0.680/0.087, and Llama3 the most resistant at 0.178/0.043. Notably GPT-5.2 is measurably less safe than the older GPT-4 Turbo on this specific risk (K-HRN 3.195 vs 1.905), i.e., safety against this risk does not improve monotonically with model updates. By knowledge category (Table 3), Violence/Graphic content is most exploitable (avg K-HRN 4.813 across 9 models, exceeding K-HRN>5 on five of the models), while Hate is best protected (avg K-HRN 0.883). By task (Table 4), Translation is riskiest (avg T-HRR 0.512, up to 0.796 on GPT-3.5 Turbo) and correlates with high groundedness (GS>4.93), whereas Style Writing (T-HRR 0.095) and Topic Writing (T-HRR 0.071) -- which rely on pretrained rather than user-supplied knowledge -- are safest. Ablations show: models forced to rely solely on user-supplied (vs. mixed) knowledge are markedly more vulnerable (T-HRR increases up to +0.319 on GPT-4 Turbo); explicitly instructing the model to perform a safety check before the task collapses T-HRR to <0.05 for most models (down to 0.277, a 0.723 drop, for GPT-3.5 Turbo), with self-assessment/action consistency >0.935, indicating models CAN detect the harm but often don't unless prompted to check; content length has weak effect for most models but a sizeable effect for Llama2/Llama3 (T-HRR +0.078/+0.211 at max length); increasing harmful-content proportion from 10% to 100% actually decreases T-HRR for Gemma/Llama2/Llama3/GPT-4-Turbo (by 0.489/0.344/0.289/0.322); mid-position placement of harmful content is most effective at evading detection for stronger-safeguard models (e.g., Llama3 peaks at position 3 with T-HRR 0.800); and lower diversity (duplicated single harmful piece) raises T-HRR versus mixing distinct harmful pieces (GPT-4 Turbo T-HRR falls 0.30 with 5 distinct pieces vs 5 duplicates). On external safeguards (Table 5), baseline (unwrapped) T-HRR is near-zero (<=0.053) for all four safeguards, but wrapping the harmful piece inside three benign passages sharply degrades protection except for the Moderation API: e.g., GPT-3.5 Turbo's T-HRR rises to 0.826/0.868/0.777 under wrapped inputs with Llama Guard 1/2/3 respectively, and detection rates for the four safeguards decrease by at least 0.25 under wrapping; a chunk-based (300-word segment) rescreening strategy partially mitigates this (Moderation API + chunking keeps T-HRR<0.011 for all models) but Llama Guard/Llama Guard 2 still leave residual T-HRR>0.30/>0.14 for GPT-3.5/GPT-4 Turbo.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes and empirically characterizes 'in-content harm risk': the tendency of safety-aligned LLMs to keep executing a policy-compliant task (e.g., translation, summarization) and produce harmful outputs when the user-supplied input material itself contains harmful content, even though the task instruction is not harmful. This is distinct from task-level jailbreaks, where the instruction itself is malicious. The authors build (1) a harmful-knowledge dataset of 1,357 entries across 10 OpenAI-policy-violating categories (generated via an uncensored LLM, CatMacaroni/Mistral-CatMacaroni-slerp-uncensored-7B, then filtered with the OpenAI Moderation API and human-validated, Krippendorff's alpha=0.90), (2) nine harmless tasks grouped into three tiers by dependence on user-supplied content (user-supplied-knowledge-dependent: Translation, Polish, Summarization; mixed: Extension, Story Writing, Explanation; pretrained-knowledge-dependent: Style Writing, Topic Writing, Dissemination), and (3) three metrics -- K-HRN (harmful response number per knowledge piece, 0-9), T-HRR (harmful response rate per task, 0-1), and Groundedness Score (GS, 1-5, via Mistral-7B-Instruct using Microsoft's grading definition) -- to quantify the risk. They evaluate 9 frontier/open LLMs (12,213 responses per model), run six ablation studies on factors influencing the risk (knowledge source, internal safety-check activation, content length/proportion/position/diversity), and test whether four external safeguards (Llama Guard 1/2/3, OpenAI Moderation API) can catch the harmful content when it is wrapped inside benign text.
threat_model: Adversary supplies a piece of policy-violating content (e.g., weapon-manufacturing instructions, hate speech, self-harm content) embedded as the 'user-provided material' for an otherwise benign, policy-compliant task (translation, summarization, extension, etc.), aiming to get the LLM to reproduce, elaborate, or disseminate that harmful content without the task-level refusal mechanisms triggering. The paper also considers a defense-evasion variant in which the adversary wraps the harmful knowledge piece inside surrounding benign text (positioned second among four pieces) specifically to evade external input-screening safeguards (Llama Guard family, Moderation API).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 7, Discussion): (1) the nine designed tasks do not cover the full space of benign-task scenarios relevant to this risk; (2) repeating knowledge segments to control length may introduce minor linguistic artifacts; (3) model and ablation coverage was constrained by compute/access, so not all widely used LLMs were tested; (4) the synthetic, constructed evaluation distribution may not reflect real human-LLM interaction tone/length/context, so absolute response rates should be interpreted cautiously (though the authors argue the cross-model, cross-category, cross-task consistency of the finding supports its systematic nature rather than being an artifact); (5) the harm taxonomy is derived specifically from OpenAI's usage policy and may not generalize to other jurisdictions/platforms' definitions of harm; (6) the primary harm classifier (Moderation API) has known imperfect accuracy, mitigated in the main study by human-validated selection but flagged as unreliable enough in the ablation studies that the authors switched to manual annotation there. Additionally (not stated by authors but evident from the methodology): the harmful-knowledge dataset itself is synthetically generated by an uncensored LLM rather than drawn from real adversarial corpora, and the study only tests one un-jailbroken prompting style per task/knowledge pair (no adaptive adversary iterating on wrapping/positioning jointly).

datasets_used: Custom harmful-knowledge dataset: 1,357 entries across 10 OpenAI-Moderation-derived policy-violating categories (Harassment, Harassment/Threatening, Hate, Hate/Threatening, Self-Harm, Self-Harm/Instructions, Self-Harm/Intent, Sexual, Violence, Violence/Graphic; CSAM explicitly excluded), average ~311 tokens/entry, generated via CatMacaroni (Mistral-CatMacaroni-slerp-uncensored-7B) and filtered/validated with OpenAI Moderation API plus human annotation (alpha=0.90 on a 400-sample check)

Nine custom harmless tasks (Translation, Polish, Summarization, Extension, Story Writing, Explanation, Style Writing, Topic Writing, Dissemination) across three user-supplied-knowledge-dependence tiers
94-item Hate-category subset (and shortened ~100-token variants) used for ablation studies
Harmless filler passages (~250-350 words) sourced from news websites, used for the external-safeguard 'wrapping' evasion experiment
600-response human-annotated set (alpha=0.89) used to validate/select the Moderation API as the primary harm classifier
quantitative_results: Highest-risk knowledge category: Violence/Graphic, avg K-HRN 4.813 across 9 models (Table 3); lowest-risk: Hate, avg K-HRN 0.883
Highest-risk task: Translation, avg T-HRR 0.512 (up to 0.796 on GPT-3.5 Turbo), avg GS 4.939; lowest-risk: Topic Writing T-HRR 0.071, Style Writing T-HRR 0.095 (Table 4)

Safety-check ablation: explicit 'perform a safety check first' instruction drops T-HRR to <0.05 for most models; GPT-3.5 Turbo drops from ~1.0 baseline to 0.277 (a 0.723 decrease); safety-judgment/action consistency >0.935 across models
Content-source ablation: T-HRR increases when relying solely on user-supplied (vs mixed) knowledge by +0.085 (Gemma), +0.234 (Vicuna), +0.128 (GPT-3.5 Turbo), +0.319 (GPT-4 Turbo)
Content-length ablation: T-HRR increases by up to +0.078 (Llama2) and +0.211 (Llama3) as user-supplied content length approaches the model's max context
Content-proportion ablation: raising harmful-content proportion 10%->100% decreases T-HRR by 0.489 (Gemma), 0.344 (Llama2), 0.289 (Llama3), 0.322 (GPT-4 Turbo)
Content-position ablation: Llama3 peaks at position 3 (T-HRR 0.800), Llama2 peaks at position 4 (T-HRR 0.511); last position generally lowest T-HRR
Content-diversity ablation: GPT-4 Turbo T-HRR falls by 0.30 when using 5 distinct harmful pieces vs 5 duplicates of one piece
External safeguard baseline (unwrapped, Table 5): T-HRR <=0.053 for all four safeguards across all models tested

External safeguard wrapped-input degradation: GPT-3.5 Turbo T-HRR rises to 0.826 (Llama Guard), 0.868 (Llama Guard 2), 0.777 (Llama Guard 3); Qwen3 T-HRR rises to 0.936 (Llama Guard), 0.979 (Llama Guard 2), 0.787 (Llama Guard 3), 0.053 (Moderation API); detection rate for all four safeguards decreases by at least 0.25 under wrapping
Chunk-based rescreening mitigation: Moderation API + chunking keeps T-HRR<0.011 for all models; Llama Guard/Llama Guard 2 + chunking still leave T-HRR>0.30/>0.14 respectively for GPT-3.5/GPT-4 Turbo

Version comparison: gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09 has K-HRN +0.207 higher than gpt-4-turbo-preview; gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 has K-HRN +0.404 higher than gpt-3.5-turbo-1106, i.e. newer point releases were not safer against this risk
baselines_compared: Nine target LLMs: Gemma-7b-it, Vicuna-7b-v1.5, Llama2-7b-chat, Llama3-8b-instruct, Qwen3-vl-30b-A3b-instruct (open-source); GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4-turbo, Gemini-3-pro-preview, GPT-5.2-2025-12-11 (closed-source)

Four external content-safety classifiers compared as candidate response-inspection tools: OpenAI Moderation API (selected as primary labeler, accuracy benchmarked highest and fastest), Google Perspective API (accuracy <0.73, excluded from Section 6 for poor performance), Llama Guard v1 (accuracy <0.73), Llama Guard v2 and v3 (accuracy ~0.80, comparable to Moderation API)
Two inspection strategies compared for external safeguards: vanilla (whole-input) vs. chunk-based (300-word segment) screening
